coredata.dc:title: 4D nanoprinted sensor for facile organo-arsenic detection: A two-photon lithography-based approach
coredata.dc:creator: Rani, Sweta
Das, Rahul Kumar
Jaiswal, Arun
Singh, Gaurav Pratap
Palwe, Ajinkya
Saxena, Sumit
Shukla, Shobha
coredata.dc:description: Engineered 3Dnanostructures have played a crucial role in the development of modern electronic devices. However, the demand for higher dimensional nanostructures have become a necessity for the development of superior next generation miniaturized devices. Traditional two-photon femtosecond laser-based fabrication techniques have enabled fabrication of 3D nanostructures. Carbon quantum dots have recently emerged as a new alternative for achieving subwavelength 3D optical lithography. Here, we report sensing of organo-arsenic(V) species at ppb levels using multimodal carbon quantum dots based 4D miniaturized ultrasensitive nanosensor and development of a device thereof using two-photon lithography. Manganese, nitrogen, and sulphur-doped carbon quantum dots have been used for development of this two-component, two-photon active functional resin for 3D printing of optically active 4D nanostructures. These have sub-wavelength resolved features, and are selectively sensitive to organo-arsenic species. The doped-carbon quantum dots initiate two-photon polymerization in the functional resin and induce emissive attributes to the polymerized nanostructures that impart selectivity towards dimethylarsinate ions (DAI) even at the ppb concentration levels. The miniaturized sensor strip shows a change in fluorescence of the nanostructures when subjected to the analyte. The emission intensities increases with increase in the concentration of DAI. The sensor can detect concentrations of the analyte as low as 1 ppb. This work opens new avenues to meet the demand for multi-dimensional nanostructures for development of superior next-generation of multifunctional miniaturized devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multidimensional nanostructures
Femto-second two-photon lithography
Polymers
Organo-arsenic fluorescence sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of acrylate and two-photon active functional (TPAF) resin
Two-photon lithography (TPL) system and fabrication process
Characterization of Mn-NS CQDs and TPAF resin-
Fabrication of a single-platform device and sensing mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing two-photon polymerization induced fabrication of Mn-NS CQD incorporated SR micro/nanostructures.

(a) Transmission electron micrograph of the synthesized Mn-NS CQDs. The inset shows histogram of particle size distribution obtained from the micrograph (b) UV–vis absorption spectra showing n → π* and π → π* transitions. (c) Photoluminescence (PL) of Mn-NS CQDs at excitations ranging from 330 nm to 500 nm. (d) Scanning electron micrograph of the structure fabricated in TPAF resin. (e) STEM image and elemental mapping of the polymerized structure, showing the uniform distribution of the constituent elements (Mn, N, S, C) present in Mn-NS CQD. (f) Atomic weight distribution of each element in the composite. (g) Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of Mn-NS CQD and sartomer incorporated with Mn-NS CQDs.

(a) and (b) AFM image and (c) height profile of the structures fabricated in TPAF resin. (d) Pseudo colour fluorescent imaging of the structures fabricated in TPAF resin at different excitations. (e-g) SEM and (h-j) fluorescent image under different excitations of complex planar hexagonal pattern, gammadion, and pyramid structures fabricated in TPAF resin.

(a) Lifetime of Mn-NS CQD upon interaction with different concentrations of DAI (b) Variation in gray-scale value of intensity of structures fabricated in TPAF resin upon interaction with different concentrations of DAI (c) response time of structures fabricated in TPAF resin upon interaction with 5 ppb of DAI (d) PL decay lifetime in the presence and absence of DAI (e) interference of different cations and anions in sensing DAI.

(a) Fabricated micro-sensor on area comparable to the area of tip of a microneedle (microneedle or needle). (b) Single-platform device for detecting DAI of various concentrations. (c) Variation of fluorescence intensity in the absence, low and high concentration of DAI. (d) Plausible mechanism of sensing DAI using TPAF micro/nano structures. The scale bars in (b) and (c) are 1 cm and 100
μ m
respectively.

High resolution scan spectra for (a) C1s, (b) Mn 2p and (c) N1s of SR@Mn-NSCQD, high resolution scan spectra for (d) Mn 2p, (e) N1s and (f) As 3d for SR@Mn-NS CQD with As (III) andhigh resolution spectra for (g) Mn 2p, (h) N1s and (i) As 3d for SR@Mn-NS CQD with DAI.
Comparison for various arsenic species sensor reported in the recent past.

head.ce:title: 4D nanoprinted sensor for facile organo-arsenic detection: A two-photon lithography-based approach

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nanostructures Engineering and Modeling Laboratory, Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai, MH 400076, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Engineered 3Dnanostructures have played a crucial role in the development of modern electronic devices. However, the demand for higher dimensional nanostructures have become a necessity for the development of superior next generation miniaturized devices. Traditional two-photon femtosecond laser-based fabrication techniques have enabled fabrication of 3D nanostructures. Carbon quantum dots have recently emerged as a new alternative for achieving subwavelength 3D optical lithography. Here, we report sensing of organo-arsenic(V) species at ppb levels using multimodal carbon quantum dots based 4D miniaturized ultrasensitive nanosensor and development of a device thereof using two-photon lithography. Manganese, nitrogen, and sulphur-doped carbon quantum dots have been used for development of this two-component, two-photon active functional resin for 3D printing of optically active 4D nanostructures. These have sub-wavelength resolved features, and are selectively sensitive to organo-arsenic species. The doped-carbon quantum dots initiate two-photon polymerization in the functional resin and induce emissive attributes to the polymerized nanostructures that impart selectivity towards dimethylarsinate ions (DAI) even at the ppb concentration levels. The miniaturized sensor strip shows a change in fluorescence of the nanostructures when subjected to the analyte. The emission intensities increases with increase in the concentration of DAI. The sensor can detect concentrations of the analyte as low as 1 ppb. This work opens new avenues to meet the demand for multi-dimensional nanostructures for development of superior next-generation of multifunctional miniaturized devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of multi-dimensional next generation of smart micro/nano analytical devices along the lines of state of the art lab-on-a-chip devices, require precisely patterned micro/nanostructures having submicron resolution on a stimuli-responsive material [1]. This can be achieved via additive manufacturing of stimuli-responsive materials, and forms a promising technique for fabrication of such miniaturised devices [2,3]. The ability to tune the properties of patterned structures [4] upon interaction with external stimuli such as temperature, magnetic field [5], pH [6], chemical species [7], and electric fields [8] have been explored in the development of such smart micro/nanodevices. A wide variety of stimuli responsive structures have been fabricated in polymer composites via photopolymerization-based additive manufacturing techniques to achieve this goal [9]. Electrically conductive structures, light-driven microrobots and light weight metamaterials with tunable mechanical properties have been fabricated using multi-walled carbon nanotubes doped polymer, UV curable shape memory polymer and photothermal shape memory hydrogels, respectively [9–11]. Although, these structures showed efficient response, their fabrication involved multistage processing and/or demanded incorporation of stimuli-specific dopants to achieve these functionalities, thereby making these techniques complex and inefficient. Photopolymerization-based printing techniques such as digital light printing, micro-stereo-lithography etc. provide diffraction limited resolution, but higher resolutions are required for further miniaturization of these smart devices. Two-photon absorption-induced polymerisation (TPIP) has been well established for single-step writing of sub-wavelength resolved structures and have emerged as a competent additive manufacturing technique [12–13] for fabrication of passive as well as responsive 2D/3D micro/nanostructures [14–19]. Commercially available polymers, photoactivated by inclusion of UV-active organic molecules such as Darocur 1173 [20], Irgacure 369 [21], Irgacure 184 [22], Lucirin-TPOL [23] etc., have commonly been used for TPIP. However, due to the small two-photon absorption (TPA) cross-section and low initiation efficiency, they require prolonged time for curing, resulting in deformation of the desired micro/nanostructures [24]. Although, the library of two-photon curable resin is expanding at a rapid pace, attaining good resolution along with the structural integrity demands development of resins exhibiting large two-photon polymerization sensitivity. It is highly dependent on the development of novel two-photon initiators (TPI) to initiate the TPIP reactions. Several synthetic organic molecules offering large values of TPA cross-section have been explored to achieve this goal, but these molecules have inherent drawbacks such as low yield, high pre/post processing toxicity, complex chemical reactions, and requirement of multiple purification stages. Recent reports indicate that nanoparticle-based quantum initiators offer a much larger TPA cross-section when compared to molecular initiators [25–28]. However, their synthesis involves the use of heavy metals, imparting cytotoxicity to such semiconductors and metal–semiconductor hybrid-based nanomaterials, thus restricting their use for several biomedical and therapeutic applications. To mitigate these issues, we have recently reported the use of hydrothermally synthesised green carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an efficient TPI, and have formulated all-carbon based material system for fabrication of fluorescent 3D micro/nanostructures [29,30]. However, the functionalization of the TPIP fabricated micro/nanostructures for specific applications is yet to be addressed.

Arsenic (As) is one of the most toxic contaminant, a global carcinogen, and poses severe threat to public health and the ecosystem [31–34]. Chronic exposure to As-contaminated water leads to severe health conditions, ranging from hyperkeratosis, skin lesions, abdominal pain to skin cancer, immunotoxic effects, neurological and cardiovascular disorders [35]. Considering the adverse effects of As-contaminated water, the development of a highly selective and portable real-time sensor for detecting ultra-trace levels of As is urgently required.

Several physical and chemical sensing techniques based on spectroscopy, colorimetric and electrochemical methods have been reported for As-detection. The instrument intensive techniques include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography, atomic fluorescence spectrometry, hydride-generation atomic absorption and emission spectrometry [30–32].

Additionally, high resolution mass spectrometers (quadrupole ion trap, quadrupole time-of-flight, Fourier transform ion cyclotron etc.) have also been used for identification of organo-arsenic compounds [36,37]. Furthermore, most marine certified reference materials are certified for dimethylarsinate (DAI) [38]. Unfortunately, these methods require sophisticated and expensive instruments, are time-consuming, need complicated sample preparation, and incur high operational costs which restricts ease of detection and incur large time lapse from sample collection to detection [27]. These are also limited in terms of selectivity and are prone to interference from other analytes [33].

Various nanomaterials such as Au nanoparticles (NPs), Fe3O4@Ag core–shell NPs, functionalized Au NPs, YPO4:Eu3+ NPs, Zn NPs, CdSe/ZnS & CdTe quantum dots (QDs), mercaptopropionic acid -capped CdTe QDs and ZnO QDs have also been explored for As detection [39]. However, these are mostly based on toxic metals/metalloids which impedes their environmental deployment and have their own drawbacks. In this context, CQDs have emerged as a preferred material, owing to their unique optical properties, biocompatibility, high stability, good quantum yield, engineered surface functionality, and facile synthesis [40]. Sensing associated with most of these methods require nanoparticles in suspended form for detection of As species [41–42], restricting their integration with the next-generation ultracompact sensors and other lab-on-a-chip devices. Current trends in the development of sensing technology have however drifted from macro and micro scale materials to the nanoscale materials for fabrication of miniaturized sensors that offers high accuracy and are energy efficient [43].

Here we report patterning of 4D ultra-sensitive As sensing nanodevice using two-photon lithography by exploiting multimodal behaviour of surface engineered CQDs. The Mn, N, and S doped-CQDs (Mn-NS CQD) initiates the polymerisation of the acrylate monomers required for TPL. These also impart fluorescence and surface functionality to the fabricated micro/nano structures for selective detection of DAI. Mn-NS CQDs were synthesised via microwave assisted pyrolysis, which resulted in amorphous, highly fluorescent and two-photon active CQDs, suitable for application in TPIP. Further, a two-photon patternable resin was formulated by adding 20 wt% of Mn-NS CQDs to an acrylate monomer. The optimised process parameters for TPIP were used to fabricate functional micro/nano structures for the detection of DAI. Mn-NS CQDs initiated two-photon polymerization reactions and the fluorescence could be retained after TPIP. The fluorescence intensity of the fabricated structures was found to increase with the concentration of DAI in aqueous medium. Fluorescence turn-on detection at ppb concentrations of analyte, suggests its potential application for printing and fabrication of 4D miniaturised sensing devices, suitable for detection of DAI in natural water bodies.

Anhydrous citric acid (Merck Ltd), thiourea (Sigma Aldrich) and manganese acetate tetrahydrate (Sigma Aldrich) were used as precursors for synthesis of Mn-NS CQDs; Tris (2-hydroxyethyl) isocyanurate acrylate (SR368) and the ethoxylated (6) trimethylolpropane triacrylate (SR499), purchased from Sartomer Co Inc., were used for preparation of the monomer resin. DAI and sodium cacodylate were purchased from Loba Chemie Ltd. Deionised (DI) water from a Milipore MilliQ System and absolute ethanol (Merck Ltd) were used as solvents. All the chemicals and reagent were used without any further purification.

Mn-NS CQDs were synthesised via a fast microwave-assisted method. Citric acid was used as the main source of carbon and, manganese acetate tetrahydrate and thiourea were used as the precursors for doping manganese, nitrogen, and sulphur. 2 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of thiourea were dissolved in 6.5 ml of DI water and 3.5 ml of 0.7 mM manganese acetate tetrahydrate was added to this solution. The resulting solution was then sonicated for 10 min. in a bath sonicator to obtain homogenous, transparent precursor solution. Subsequently, the mixture was exposed to 490 W microwave operating at 2.45 GHz for 15 min. to 180 °C and a dark brown solid is obtained. 10 ml of DI water was then added to the solid and stirred for 10 min. The brown solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through a
0.22 μ m
membrane filter. This solution was lyophilised to obtain powdered Mn-NS CQDs. The schematic for Mn-NS CQD synthesis is shown as supplementary Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of acrylate and two-photon active functional (TPAF) resin
ce:section.ce:para: Sartomer (SR) solution was prepared by mixing SR368 and SR499 in 1:1 wt ratio. Two-photon active functional (TPAF) resin was prepared by adding 20 wt% of Mn-NS CQD to the SR solution. The resultant mixture was then sonicated for 2 h to ensure uniform mixing of Mn-NS CQDs in the SR solution. The as-prepared resin was stored in a vacuum desiccator for further use. The degree of agglomeration of the quantum dots in the TPAF resin was observed to be negligible. The optical properties of the TPAF resin remained unmodified for 6 months and the Mn-NS CQDs remained uniformly suspended.

The quantum yield of the manganese, nitrogen, sulphur co-doped CQDs was calculated using equation (1).
(1)
Q Y
s a m p l e
= Q Y
r e f e r e n c e
×
A b s
( r e f e r e n c e )
A b s
( s a m p l e )
×
A
s a m p l e
A ( r e f e r e n c e )
×
η
s a m p l e
η
r e f e r e n c e
where
QY: Quantum yield; Abs: Absorbance; A: Slope (PL integrated area vs UV absorbance); ɳ: refractive index of solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two-photon lithography (TPL) system and fabrication process
ce:section.ce:para:
An in-house developed TPL system was used for the fabrication of micro/nanostructures in the TPAF resin. In this set-up, femto-second laser (Coherent: Chameleon, Ultra I), operating at a wavelength of 800 nm, having 140 fs pulse duration and 80 MHz repetition rate was coupled to an inverted microscope.
25 μ l
of the TPAF resin was drop-casted on pre-cleaned glass coverslip and spin-coated at 4000 rpm. This was mounted on the motorized stage and then exposed to femtosecond laser. A 100X, 0.9NA objective was used to focus the laser beam tightly into the resin. The laser focus was kept constant, and the resin-coated glass coverslip was traversed using the assembly of motorized stage to fabricate the desired micro/nano structures. The unexposed resin was then washed off using dimethyl formamide (DMF).

The ion sensing was performed by exposing the Mn-NS CQD incorporated micro/nano structures to different DAI ion concentrations (1, 5, 20, 100, 450 and 750 ppb). The concentrations maintained under the study were kept for varying time intervals (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 and 35 min) to analyse the response time of the sensor. Additionally, the selectivity test of sensor towards DAI was conducted using different common ions; cations (Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+and Fe3+) and anions (F-, Cl-, Br-and NO3 –) usually present in natural water bodies.

The optical properties of the Mn-NS CQDs were studied using UV − VIS absorption spectroscopy (Shimadzu UV 2600) and photoluminescence (Shimadzu RF2400). The morphology and size distribution of Mn-NS CQD were analysed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL FEG-TEM 200 kV). Surface functionalization of the Mn-NS CQD and TPAF resin were determined by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker 3000 Hyperion). The fabricated structures were characterised using a confocal optical microscopy (Olympus IX81) and environmental scanning electron microscopy (ESEM, FEI QUANTA 200). The fluorescence emission intensities of the TPAF resin and the fabricated micro/nano structures, at different excitations, was analysed by single-photon fluorescent imaging using the confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Carl Zeiss, LSM 780). The sensing mechanism, based on the interaction between SR@Mn-NS CQD and DAI, was analysed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Kratos Analytical, AXIS Supra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Mn-NS CQDs and TPAF resin-
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the schematic of the TPL-based fabrication of responsive micro/nanostructures using TPAF resin, prepared as described in section 2.3. The functional Mn-NS CQDs were synthesised by microwave-assisted pyrolysis, as described in section 2.2.

The morphology of Mn-NS CQD was analyzed using TEM micrographs. They exhibit a quasi-spherical structure (Fig. 2 a) with size ranging within 2 to 12 nm, as shown in the particle size distribution histogram (inset of Fig. 2a).

The optical properties of Mn-NS CQD were studied using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The absorption peaks (Fig. 2b) of Mn-NS CQDs were observed at 282 (λmax), 337 and 445 nm, respectively. The peak at 282 nm can be attributed to
π -
π
∗
transition in the
C = C
network of planar carbon moieties where as the peak at 337 nm originates due to the
n -
π
∗
transition of
C = O
as well as Mn, N, and S, related surface defects. The enhanced absorbance at 445 nm, which was absent in the case of all-carbon CQDs (Fig. S2),suggests the presence of various surface functionalities developed during the synthesis of doped-CQDs. Mn-NS CQDs exhibit excitation dependent emission (Fig. 2c) alike standard quantum confined dots [44], which undergoes bathochromic shift with increase in excitation wavelength from UV to visible region (330–500 nm). This indicates the presence of various functional groups (carboxylic acid, keto-, amine, amide, thiol, sulphonic acid) on the surface of the CQDs, therein, providing the scope to integrate and engineer different types of electronic transitions at the nanoscale on CQDs, making them versatile in comparison to organic dyes or inorganic quantum dots and enable in inception of new possibilities for 4D fabrication. The quantum yield of synthesised CQDs was estimated to be 11.2 % using equation (1).

The TPAF resin was then synthesized as described in section 2.5 and used to fabricate micro/nanostructures. Considering the printing efficiency and the final performance of the sensor, 20 wt% of Mn-NS CQD was chosen for preparing the TPAF resin. This facilitated the fabrication of micro/nano structures in TPAF resin at a lower threshold power and also ensured a uniform distribution of Mn-NS CQD in the fabricated structures for optimized sensing performance. All the structures were fabricated under constant average laser exposure of 320 mW at a scanning speed of
100
μ m / s .
The SEM micrograph of the nano-cage structure fabricated in the TPAF resin is shown in Fig. 2d. The presence of carbon, nitrogen, sulphur and manganese in the polymerized micro/nanostructure was verified by STEM imaging (Fig. 2e). Uniform distribution for all elements was observed in the elementally mapped micrographs. EDAX analysis of SR@Mn-NS CQD with atomic weight distribution of each element in the composite is shown in Fig. 2f. The atomic weight % for C, N, O, S, and Mn was found to be 79.04, 5.85, 6.41, 0.55 and 0.08 %, respectively.

The surface functionality of the material was studied by using vibrational spectroscopy and binding energy evaluation. FTIR study (Fig. 2g) for SR and Mn-NS CQD-incorporated SR structure exhibits the presence of Mn-O (611 cm−1), -SO3-(bending/1222 cm−1), CO (stretch/1338 cm−1), CO (stretch/1742 cm−1), NC = S (stretch, 2080 cm−1), SH (stretch/2744 cm−1) and NH/OH (stretch/3420 cm−1) surface functional groups. The broad 3420 cm−1 peak (Mn-NS CQD) narrows down in Mn-NS CQD incorporated SR due to the decrease of hydrogen bonding in amine and hydroxyl group after incorporation into SR matrix. The slight shift of NC = S stretch of Mn-NS CQD to lower wavenumber in Mn-NS doped SR resin indicates bonding of Mn-NS CQDs with the SR matrix.

AFM analysis was performed to evaluate the height and surface roughness of the micro/nano structures fabricated in TPAF resin, (Fig. 3 a-c). The height of the grating was observed to be uniform (0.223 μm) throughout the structure having a surface roughness of 7.73 nm. These observations confirm the high quality of the constructs. Confocal fluorescent image of the fabricated nano-cage structure under an excitation of bright light, 405, 488 and 561 nm shows bright fluorescence (Fig. 3d), owing to the uniform distribution of fluorescent Mn-NS CQDs in the polymerised micro/nano structures. The average emission intensity (Fig. S4) extracted from the fluorescent micro/nanostructures indicates the uniform distribution of Mn-NS CQDs in the polymerized structures. This leads to a high signal to background noise ratio and a uniform emission throughout the polymerized micro/nanostructure. Complex planar and three-dimensional structures have also been fabricated in the TPAF resin. SEM micrographs (Fig. 3e-g) of the fabricated structures i.e. hexagonal pattern, gammadion and pyramid, demonstrate the ability to fabricate high-quality, complex micro/nanostructures in the developed resin. These structures exhibited strong emissions at an excitation of 488 nm (Fig. 3h-j). The ease of fabrication of complex micro/nano structures in TPAF resin suggests its suitability for fabrication of miniaturised devices.

Fluorescence based sensing of DAI has been explored here using TPIP fabricated micro/nano structures in TPAF resin. As reported in literature, the particle density in photo-polymerized resin doped with QDs is more as compared to the films/solution of QD [25]. Further, the photo-polymerization-driven shrinkage of acrylate films also leads to increased particle density within the polymerised structures. These two factors contribute to large enhancement of fluorescence in the photo-polymerized structures as compared to solution/unpolymerized films (Fig. S5). We have utilised this phenomenon for sensing low concentrations of DAI. The nano-cage structures fabricated with TPAF resin, exhibits emission corresponding to the different excitation wavelengths. Gray scale value of the intensity profile shows an increase in the PL emission with increase in DAI concentration, at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm. The mechanism of arsenic sensing by Mn-NS CQD was understood using PL-lifetime measurement for different analyte amounts in aqueous medium (Fig. 4 a).

It was observed that with increasing analyte ions, the average lifetime of fluorescence shifted from 1.8 ns to 3.12 ns. This indicates that the radiative decay path for quantum dot was delayed by the interaction of arsenic ion with Mn-NS CQD surface. Further, the PL intensity of the structures fabricated in TPAF resin was found to increase from 15.2 to 40.3 as DAI concentration was increased from 1 ppb to 750 ppb (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c exhibits the response time by TPAF nano-cage structures upon addition of 5 ppb analyte solution. The fluorescence emission was found to saturate at 25 min for the TPAF resin-based micro-sensor. Upon addition of 5 ppb DAI, enhanced and red-shifted PL was observed in the decay lifetime, and an increase in average lifetime of Mn-NS CQD solution was observed upon interaction with increasing concentrations of Arsenic (Fig. S6), thus, supporting the changes observed in time-resolved photoluminescence spectrum of two-photon fabricated micro device, confirming the detection of DAI ion (Fig. 4d. To verify the selectivity of miniaturized sensor, the microstructure was exposed to different ions (cations and anions) in aqueous medium (Fig. 4e). The concentration of these ions was maintained at 500 ppb for the interference analysis. A negligible interference was observed in the presence of cation Hg2+ and anions F-, NO3 –, Br- ions. The limit of detection for the fabricated micro-sensor towards analyte was calculated to be 0.22 ppb (Fig. S7). Table 1 shows a comparative of reports which exhibits mostly gold based composites. Sensing of ionic forms of arsenic (As (III), As (V)),higher limit of detection and requires sophisticated instruments [45–51]. In comparison to this, the sensor device fabricated in this work beholds potential for further development for mobile, cost-effective analytical tool capable of fulfilling features associated to a real-time, nano-sensing device. Sensing using CQDs have been largely achieved in solution medium whereas our device nurtures a solid CQD based portable platform for arsenic (DAI) detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of a single-platform device and sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
Different types of on-chip devices with unique advantages have been proposed [52,53]. In this context, micro-sensor over an area (
100
μ m
by
100
μ m
), comparable to the width of the microneedles (micro needles or needles) (Fig. 5 a), were fabricated. For better sensing ability multiple micro-sensing structures on a single-platform are provided. These are physically isolated from each other (Fig. 5b). Different concentrations of DAI were added in each cove (Video S5), which yielded different intensity of PL emissions at an excitation of 488 nm. The intensity was observed to be lowest in the absence of DAI and was observed to increase with the increasing concentration of DAI (Fig. 5c).

The retained fluorescence in all the structures enable PL sensing of DAI ions. The proposed material system is the first report on Mn-NS CQD based polymerisation of sartomer for fabricating functional micro/nanostructures and its application in organo-arsenic sensing. The fabricated single-platform device is the 3D printed construct whose “chemical state” changes with respect to external stimuli (DAI concentration present in water) over time and shows in the form of variation of emissivity/fluorescence intensity of the 3D construct. The emissive property of the entire 3D construct is the functionality that varies under the influence of external stimuli. This approach can be further extended for generation of miniaturized, 4D sensing and actuation nano-platforms.

Plausible sensing mechanism (Fig. 5d) for our system was derived based on analyte ion interaction with TPAF nano-cage structures.
(2)
A s
V
C a c o d y l a t e
i o n
+
2 e
-
M n - N S
C Q D @ S R
→
A s
I I I
(3)
2 M n O
2
+
A s
( I I I )
+
H 2
O →
2 M n O O H
∗
+
A s
( V )
+
2 H
+
(4)
2 M n O O H
∗
+
A s
( I I I )
+
2 H
+
→
2 M n
2 +
+
A s
( V )
+
3 H
2
O

At first, the cacodylate ion gains free electrons from Mn-NS CQD surface and gets reduced from As(V) to As(III). Mn oxidises As(III) back to As(V) by reducing itself to MnOOH* [54]. Thus, the vicinity of As ions induce electron density in the quantum confined Mn-NS CQD resulting in enhanced fluorescence intensity of the overall nano-cage with increasing DAI concentration. Other synergistic binding sites based on hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions are also possible due to functionalities available on both analyte and sensor.

XPS analysis of the SR@Mn-NS CQD with DAI and As (III) enables in understanding the sensing mechanism for fluorescence based DAI detection. C1s, N1s, Mn2p, S2p, O1s and As3d high resolution scans were performed to investigate binding energy profiles for SR@Mn-NS CQD. Fig. 6 a, S8c, S8g shows binding energy for C1s for SR@Mn-NS CQD(284.1 eV), SR@Mn-NS CQD with As(III) ion (285.8 eV) and SR@Mn-NS CQD with DAI (285.4 eV), which can be attributed to the co-ordination of CN, CO and CS bonds toward As (III) as well as DAI ion [44]. Similarly, correlative analysis for Mn 2p (Fig. 6b, 6d, 6 g) exhibits three satellite peaks (641.8, 642.5, 644.1 eV) for DAI with SR@Mn-NS CQD confirming new binding energies upon interaction with analyte under aqueous conditions [55]. Further, the ratio of pyrrolic N in N1s peaks (Fig. 6c, 6e, 6 h) were found to decrease in ratio to pyridinic N upon interaction with As (III) and DAI, which can be attributed to the binding of pyrrolic N with analytes [56]. The analysis also focused on higher degree of pyrrolic N binding in case of DAI when compared to As (III). In case of O1s peaks (Supplementary Fig. S8a, S8d, S8g), a significant shift of COC peak in SR@Mn-NS CQD from 530.6 eV to 531.3 eV after sensing both analyte suggests its contribution in chemical interaction with the sensor matrix. The sulphur (Supplementary Fig. S8c) was found to be majorly in its oxidized form (sulfonic acid groups) [57]. Additionally, a considerable decrease in the OC-O functionality peak (532.1 eV) in Supplementray Fig. S8d and S8g explains the role of carboxylic acid groups in the SR@Mn-NSCQD-analyte interfacial interactions. As (III) ion displays a strong binding with sulphur of the sensor, when compared to DAI with SR@Mn-NS CQD (Fig S8b, S8e and S8h). The high resolution scans for As 3d in both SR@Mn-NSCQD with As (III) (Fig. 6f) and with DAI (Fig. 6i), an enhanced peak at 43.4 eV supports the reduction of As (V) to As (III) on the surface of SR@Mn-NS CQDs [58]. The other peaks (41.8, 44.2 and 45.7 eV) were in accordance to the As (V) as well as As (III) 5/2 and 3/2 respectively. Overall, it can be observed that the fabricated sensor matrix with specific surface functionalities exhibit more affinity towards the fluorescence turn on based sensing of DAI.

In summary, we have developed a miniaturised 4D platform for organo-arsenic sensing via two-photon lithography of the synthesized functional resin. This active resin exploits the multimodal behaviour of the Mn-NS CQDs to eliminate the use of any other two-photon initiator/dye for initiating two-photon initiated polymerization reactions. Furthermore, no additional dopant is required to impart selective detection towards the analyte. The surface engineering of the microwave pyrolyzed Mn-NS CQD has enabled multi-functional traits into it. It not only serves as a photoinitiator for TPIP but also imparts fluorescence to the polymerised micro/nano structures thus enabling efficient sensing. The binding of N and S of Mn-NS CQD with O and N functionalities of SR confines intact incorporation of Mn-NS CQDs in the polymer matrix. The retained fluorescence in nano-cage and other 3D structure confirms the presence of free surface states on Mn-NS CQDs even after polymerisation. The combination of manganese, nitrogen and sulphur in sensor microstructure suits impeccably with the requirements for quick arsenic detection. The LOD for the developed sensor was found to be 0.22 ppb, which is well acceptable for application towards next generation sensing devices. The proposed methodology is expected to induce rapid progress towards development of 4D nanostructures for efficient sensing and detection platforms having extended functionalities.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their gratitude to the Technology Mission Division, Department of Science and Technology (DST), Ministry of Science and Technology, India through grant no. DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/100(C) and Science and Engineering Research Board, India grant no. EMR/2017/005144 for partial financial support.
